required_edits: code.mjs: delete the private resolveModulePath and resolve relative import specifiers through util's exported resolveRelativeSpecifier (or a thin wrapper it exports) so exactly ONE resolver exists; keep the code-weaver's 'seeded file below closed roots' membership test as the extraction-grammar filter, but the specifier->path resolution must be the shared function. Strengthen the AM-34 test-19 unit so it cannot be satisfied by renaming: assert code.mjs's import list includes the shared resolver, or assert no OTHER function in clotho/ performs join+normalize+extension-append resolution.

test-closure.mjs section 1: derive the real weaver closures with allowExternal = new Set(PERMITTED_EXTERNAL_CLOSURE_FILES) so the committed permitted-external policy is the SAME policy used in the normative equality check.

test-closure.mjs section 5: replace the hand-array notDeepEqual assertions with an actual inventory-equality harness (the same comparison the real check uses) and add the spec-enumerated per-form omission fixtures: an inventory omitting a member reachable ONLY via export-from/export-star, ONLY via literal dynamic import(), and ONLY via require()/module.require(), each proven to FAIL equality; plus an extra-file inventory and a nonexistent-file inventory failing through the same harness (not via a bare statSync throw).

util.mjs classifyModuleLoads: bind each from-clause to its governing import/export by scanning forward from the keyword (bounded clause parse over masked source) instead of nearest-preceding-keyword over the whole head; add units for adjacent dynamic-import-then-static-import, multiple declarations on one line, and `export * as ns from` explicitly (the recognized namespace re-export variant D27/D33 names), asserting correct per-site form attribution.

resolveRelativeSpecifier: enforce the extension rule as part of the shared resolver (reject or classify non-.mjs resolved targets per the frozen extension rules) OR document-and-test the accepted extension set with explicit units, so closure membership for non-.mjs files is a decided, tested behavior rather than an accident of candidate ordering.

test-git.mjs: add a unit asserting the -S argument is passed as a SINGLE argv token `-S<symbol>` with a symbol containing regex metacharacters (e.g. `a$b.c`) and that the mock receives it verbatim — the metacharacter-safe matching obligation covers the git path too, and validateGitArgs already permits it; the exact-shape proof for metacharacter symbols is currently absent.
hard_stops: WEAVER_IMPL_FILES for clotho-git-weaver omits clotho/inventory.mjs and clotho/weavers/util.mjs style shared substrate ONLY IF actually imported — verify: git.mjs imports ../registry.mjs only, so closure {registry.mjs, git.mjs} appears correct; HOWEVER the closure derivation test (test-closure.mjs section 1) calls deriveAcceptedClosure WITHOUT passing allowExternal built from PERMITTED_EXTERNAL_CLOSURE_FILES, meaning a permitted merkle-dag import in a real weaver would be reported 'forbidden' rather than traversed — the real-inventory equality check does not exercise the committed permitted-external list and would fail for a legitimate future closure; more critically it proves the shared resolver is invoked with different policy inputs in the normative check vs. the spec's stated closure definition

Task 4a requires closure-equality failure fixtures proving that 'an inventory missing a closure file — including one reached only through re-export, literal dynamic import, or an accepted require-style form — an inventory listing an extra file, and an inventory naming a nonexistent file all FAIL'. test-closure.mjs section 5 only asserts assert.notDeepEqual on hand-built arrays and that statSync throws ENOENT on a nonexistent path — it never exercises an actual inventory-vs-closure comparison failure path for the form-specific missing-file cases (re-export-only, dynamic-import-only, require-only members omitted from a committed-style inventory). The spec text explicitly enumerates these per-form omission fixtures; they are absent.

classifyModuleLoads misclassifies `export * as ns from "./x.mjs"` style recognized namespace re-export variants only via the /\*/ heuristic on the region after the LAST import/export keyword; worse, the from-clause classifier attributes a from-clause to the nearest PRECEDING import/export keyword anywhere earlier in the file — e.g. a file containing `export const a = 1;` followed later by a stray `from "./x.mjs"` inside otherwise-masked code, or an import keyword consumed by a prior clause, can misattribute or fabricate a load site. Example defect: for source `import("./a.mjs"); const y = from_table; import { b } from "./b.mjs";` the nearest-preceding-keyword scan can bind the second from-clause to the dynamic-import keyword. The classifier also treats ANY `from "..."` token sequence as a load site whenever any import/export keyword appears anywhere earlier — e.g. `const q = 1; export const from_ = 2;` plus a later object literal `{ from: "./z.mjs" }`... the (?<![A-Za-z0-9_$])from guard blocks `from:` key matches only when not followed by whitespace-quote; `{ from "./z.mjs" }` is invalid JS but `case from "x"` is not the issue — the substantive risk is misattribution between import/export governing keywords, which changes form classification (import vs export-star vs export-from) and thus whether the closure traverses or the scanner accepts. This is a correctness defect in the ONE shared D33 classifier that both the closure and the future advisory scanner must trust.

resolveRelativeSpecifier extension rule diverges from spec: it appends .mjs for ANY extensionless specifier, and accepts a resolved existing file of ANY extension (e.g. './helper.js', './x.json') as a closure member — the frozen scope defines extension rules as part of the ONE shared resolver but code.mjs separately enforces 'explicit .mjs target' semantics with its OWN lexical resolveModulePath (dir-join + normalize + append .mjs), creating exactly the second resolver D33 forbids ('no closure-only form list and NO closure-only resolver — one relative-specifier resolver'). code.mjs does not import resolveRelativeSpecifier; it defines resolveModulePath with different containment (lexical, no lstat, no symlink checks) and different extension behavior. The spec's knowledge-graph grammar is allowed to differ in WHAT it extracts, but the resolution of a relative specifier to a file is the resolver util exports, and AM-34 test 19's intent — one classifier, one resolver, both consumers — is contradicted in the very PR that introduces it. Note test-closure section 9 only greps for functions NAMED resolveRelativeSpecifier, so the duplicate resolver under a different name passes the unit while violating the invariant it claims to prove.
rationale: The implementation is substantially faithful in most dimensions I checked: the frozen REQUIRED_INVENTORY_IDS table matches the normative table exactly with an equality unit; the D32 mapping is exact, deep-frozen, with deep-equal/key/value/mutation units; D17/AM-17 is respected (only git+code inventories, all named files exist, LEDGER_SOURCES intentionally empty); the counted iterator has correct completion-only counting semantics with the right units (partial not counted, break not exhausted, cardinality, driver-held accessor only); physicalContainment lstats every component and never follows symlinks, with junction-based units; the git wrapper is a strict no-shell allowlist over exactly the five shapes; git.mjs emits the exact commanded log invocations, validates every line as full SHA (malformed fatal), takes first result, source_ref git:<sha>, warns on empty; code.mjs covers all four endpoint shapes, preserves no-export named imports as repository-file->code-symbol, dedups, ignores unused/comment/string uses, and confines unrepresentable-consumer to unresolvable specifiers; determinism is byte-proven. However I cannot sign it as-is on four genuine faithfulness defects. (1) D33's core discipline \u2014 ONE resolver shared by both consumers \u2014 is violated inside this very PR: code.mjs ships its own lexical resolveModulePath (different containment, different extension handling) instead of importing the shared resolver; the AM-34 test-19 unit greps only for the canonical names, so it passes while the invariant it exists to prove is false in spirit. The task text does say the code-weaver's extraction grammar is unchanged by D33, but specifier->file resolution is precisely what the shared resolver exists for, and two resolvers is the named forbidden condition. (2) The normative real-inventory equality check derives closures without the committed PERMITTED_EXTERNAL_CLOSURE_FILES policy, so the check as-written enforces a different policy than the committed one (latent false-fatal for the first legitimate merkle-dag import, and it means the committed permitted-external list is untested against the real derivation path). (3) The spec explicitly enumerates closure-equality FAILURE fixtures per accepted form (missing member reachable only via re-export / dynamic import / require-style); section 5 substitutes hand-built notDeepEqual arrays and a raw ENOENT statSync, which does not exercise the actual failure path of the equality check for those forms. (4) The from-clause classifier's nearest-preceding-keyword binding is structurally fragile and can misattribute forms between adjacent load sites; since this is the ONE classifier both the closure and future scanner must trust, its attribution logic needs to be clause-bounded and unit-pinned, including the `export * as ns from` variant the plan names explicitly. None of these require scope expansion to fix \u2014 they are corrections to bring the artifact to what Task 4a already says. Gate green does not cover them: the gate checks existence/needles/exit codes, not resolver unification or fixture completeness. Revise, not reject: the architecture is right, the defects are localized and repairable within frozen scope.
